column2: The project would build 1.2 miles of new six-lane freeway to complete State Route 252, connecting Interstate 5 and Interstate 805 in Southeast San Diego. It includes overcrossings at 38th, 40th, and 43rd Streets, a pedestrian bridge to Southcrest Park, an I-5/Route 252 connector, and realignment of the South Chollas Creek channel. Right of way of about 44 acres was already purchased and cleared, with remaining construction estimated at $12.4 million and tentatively scheduled to begin in 1977.
column7: The extraction is largely accurate and well-supported by the cited pages. The specific figure of '4,300 accidents prevented' does not appear in the visible cited text and may be drawn from a page not…

column2: The freeway would cross Southeast San Diego, a low-income, ethnically diverse community of about 50,000 residents that is roughly 48% Afro-American, 29% Mexican-American, 16% Anglo, and 6% Asian-American. Census Tract 36, which the route crosses, has a median income well below the citywide figure and 24% of families below the poverty level. The neighborhood is already bordered or divided by Interstates 5, 15, 805, and Route 94, and about 280 housing units have already been removed and their occupants relocated for the right of way.

Alternatives considered include a no-build option, widening Division Street and other local arterials (which would displace about 240 structures), widening Interstates 5 and Route 94 to ten lanes, extending Route 252 ramps to Division Street for more local service, ramp metering, downgrading Route 252 to a non-freeway facility, exclusive busway lanes, and mass transit or fixed guideway options. A 1965 hearing also weighed a second alignment that was rejected as more costly and disruptive. A 1974 regional planning report concluded that doing nothing would not meet traffic and environmental sta…

The project commits about 44 acres of urban land and 0.15 acre of Southcrest Park to freeway use and would further physically and psychologically divide the community with a 350-foot-wide corridor. Traffic noise would exceed 70 dBA L10 at about ten homes, seven commercial buildings along 43rd Street, and the southern part of Southcrest Park, while sound mounds and a depressed profile reduce noise near the park. Carbon monoxide levels would stay well below national standards, and the project is projected to cut regional vehicle miles traveled by tens of thousands per day, lowering overall emis…

After a 1965 hearing the route was adopted with support from the City of San Diego and the school district, and it was included in the 1969 Southeast San Diego Community Plan. In November 1973 the Southeast San Diego Development Committee reversed course and opposed the freeway, citing community division, weak local service, pollution, noise, and cost, but the City Council reaffirmed building the route in February 1975. The City's Environmental Quality Department, regional planning agency, EPA, UMTA, the Clean Air Council, and the Model Cities Citizens' Advisory Board raised concerns or oppos…
